Title: I couldn't help myself, so it's not really my fault, right?
Desc:
Why do I see Karenina everywhere?
But really, who wouldn't want to take a look—or two, or many—when they see an Engineering Force captain selling milk tea?
I couldn't help myself, so it's not really my fault, right?
My database must be overloaded with too many of her "highlights," making her appear whenever I look.
Tch, time to clear my collection.
Since she did give me a little "hint," I picked one that's not too embarrassing for you!
Feel free to show it to her—I'm looking forward to her reaction.

Title: Consciousness Tide Dossier
Desc:
World Government Science Council
Dossier ID: C-3057-74A
Classification: Level III (Laboratory Directors and above)
Issue Date: July 4, 2157

Subject: Additional notes on the "Consciousness Tide" theory and Construct M.I.N.D. technology
Over the past decade, the Arius Erhorn team has found that early primate communities once shared a highly coordinated collective unconscious.
Simulations show that even without direct contact, individuals could sync emotions and sense danger together. The data pattern behaves like an ocean—vast, dynamic, and deeply connected—so the council named it the "Consciousness Tide," sometimes represented by the symbol ▄▇█.
Its fluctuations follow changes in human collective emotion and show clear non-linear, cyclical behavior.
The M.I.N.D. system used in Constructs is modeled after the human limbic system and default network. It mirrors human emotional processing. Highly rational M.I.N.D.s can stay logically stable, but under heavy ▄▄▇██ pressure, they may fluctuate or even collapse.
Research from Dominik and Professor Erhorn suggests that the Consciousness Tide contains several stable, high-density information nodes. These nodes match basic human emotions—joy, anger, sorrow, fear—and act as anchor points within the field of consciousness.
If Constructs can safely connect to these anchors, they may gain support similar to ancient collective unconscious systems. Shared emotional resonance could help distribute mental stress and keep their M.I.N.D. stable.
It is important to note that this research is still high-risk. We do not yet understand how emotional anchor points form, and external access may cause reverse contamination. Fossil records show that the decline of the collective unconscious and the rise of isolated individual minds happened at the same time in evolution, suggesting that forcing deep reconnection may come with irreversible costs.
The following sections, which include Sefirah extraction protocols and M.I.N.D. stability data, are encrypted under Council Resolution No. 118.
Decryption requires joint approval from the Chief Technician and the Ethics Committee.
